codeReview.summary: The feature adds in-memory throttling, fallback-provider plumbing, and degraded Redis logging, but the worker wiring does not actually route provider selection from effective project/group policy, so the core provider-routing requirement is not implemented end-to-end.

issues.description: The worker hard-codes the primary/secondary provider routes as `default` and `secondary` and always passes the same fallback provider into `llm.NewProcessor`, but nothing threads `rules.Load(...).EffectivePolicy.ProviderRoute` into provider selection. As a result, project/group policy cannot choose the provider route used for a run, so the implementation does not satisfy the feature's required route-selection behavior.

`TestProviderRouteSelection` only asserts that a fake rules loader returns `EffectivePolicy.ProviderRoute`; it does not verify that processor/runtime code actually uses that policy to select a provider. This leaves the missing end-to-end route-selection behavior untested and allowed the blocking gap above to ship.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The feature is tagged with `go-reliability`, but its handoff says the worker followed procedure from a different integration-oriented flow, and the added route-selection test only checked rules metadata instead of exercising the runtime contract. The current skill guidance may not be pushing workers to verify end-to-end policy-to-runtime wiring for mixed reliability/integration features.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: features.json marks `beta-provider-routing-rate-limits` with `skillName: go-reliability`, while the implementation coverage in internal/llm/provider_test.go:150-157 only inspects `rules.LoadResult.EffectivePolicy.ProviderRoute` and never proves provider selection in cmd/worker/main.go or llm.Processor.

summary: Review failed: throttling and fallback pieces landed, but effective project/group provider routing is not wired into the runtime, so the feature does not fully meet its expected behavior.
